title: Create a Group and Assign Permissions with AWS IAM
content:
In this section, we will create an IAM User Group and assign appropriate access permissions to it.
Instead of assigning permissions directly to individual users, AWS recommends managing permissions through Groups to improve scalability and follow the Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) model.
Objectives After completing this step, you will:
Create an IAM User Group Attach the AmazonS3ReadOnlyAccess policy to the group Understand how groups centrally manage permissions for multiple users Step 1: Create a User Group & Attach Policy Navigate to:
IAM User groups Create user group Here, enter the group name. In this lab, the group is named: test-policy
Search for the policy: AmazonS3ReadOnlyAccess Step 2: Create the Group After successful creation, the group test-policy will appear in the list. Step 3: Verify Group Permissions Navigate to: IAM > User groups > test-policy > Permissions Verify that the policy has been attached:

title: Introduction
content:
Workshop Overview In this workshop, you will learn how to control access to AWS resources using AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM). IAM is a core AWS service that enables secure and fine-grained access control for users and services.
Instead of assigning permissions directly to individual users, this workshop demonstrates how to manage access more effectively using User Groups and Policies, following AWS best practices.
Objectives After completing this workshop, you will be able to:
Create and manage IAM Users Create User Groups to represent roles Assign permissions using IAM Policies Understand how users inherit permissions from groups Validate access control using Amazon S3 Main Activities During this workshop, you will perform the following steps:
Create a new IAM User Create a User Group and assign a policy Add the user to the group Log in using the created IAM user Access the Amazon S3 service Test different actions: Allowed actions (Allow) Denied actions (Access Denied) Architecture Model This workshop follows a simple access control model:
User → Group → Policy → Resource Where:
User represents an individual identity Group represents a role Policy defines permissions Resource is the AWS service (e.g., S3 Bucket) RBAC in AWS IAM This workshop applies the Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) model:
Permissions are assigned to groups (roles) Users are assigned to groups Users inherit permissions from those groups This approach provides:
Centralized permission management Better scalability Reduced configuration errors Alignment with AWS security best practices Expected Outcomes By the end of this workshop, you will:
Understand how IAM works in real-world scenarios Build a basic access control system using AWS IAM Clearly distinguish between Allow and Access Denied Apply the Least Privilege principle in practice This workshop not only covers theoretical concepts but also provides hands-on experience by validating access behavior directly on AWS services.

title: Event 1
content:
Summary Report: “AWS re:Invent 2025 Recap (Vietnam)” Event Information Item Details Event Name AWS re:Invent 2025 Recap (Vietnam) Date & Time 27/01/2026 Location AWS Vietnam Office (Floor 26 & 36), Ho Chi Minh City Role Attendee (FCJ Cloud Intern) Event Objectives Review key announcements and innovations from AWS re:Invent 2025 Explore Generative AI advancements, especially Agentic AI and Amazon Bedrock Understand new approaches to data storage and AI/ML infrastructure Connect with AWS Solution Architects and the local tech community Speakers Mr. Thi – Solution Architect
Topic: Generative AI & Agents Mr. Tung – Speaker
Topic: OpenSearch & Agentic Search AWS Solution Architects and Account Managers Key Highlights Generative AI and Agentic Systems Introduction to Amazon Nova Models, AWS’s high-performance foundation models Exploration of Bedrock Agents, including orchestration, flows, memory, and guardrails Understanding Agentic AI, where systems move beyond simple responses to executing multi-step workflows SageMaker and Data Innovations SageMaker Unified Studio: a collaborative environment for data engineers, scientists, and AI developers S3 Tables: native support for Apache Iceberg format S3 Vector: enables storing vector data directly in S3 with lower cost Search and Multimodal AI OpenSearch Serverless integrated with AI-driven workflows Nova Multimodal Embeddings for converting images and videos into vectors Bedrock Data Automation for extracting insights from multimedia data AI/ML Infrastructure SageMaker HyperPod for managing large-scale GPU clusters SageMaker MLflow for end-to-end ML lifecycle management Key Highlights (Standout Services) S3 Vector – Cost-efficient Vector Storage Enables vector storage directly in S3, reducing infrastructure cost Suitable for RAG systems and log analysis use cases Simplifies architecture compared to dedicated vector databases SageMaker Unified Studio – Improved Collaboration Consolidates data preparation, experimentation, and model development Enhances team productivity by reducing tool fragmentation Supports better collaboration across different roles Key Takeaways Shift Toward Agent Engineering AI is evolving from prompt-based interaction to agent-based systems Agents can execute workflows, maintain context, and integrate with external tools Guardrails are essential to ensure reliability and safe operation Data and Compute Optimization S3 Vector provides a cost-effective solution for vector-based systems, especially under budget constraints
Reduces reliance on specialized vector databases and simplifies system design
SageMaker Unified Studio enhances collaboration by unifying multiple development stages into one workspace
Improves efficiency and reduces operational complexity
AWS as a Complete AI Ecosystem AWS offers a full stack from data processing to deployment Enables scalable and production-ready AI solutions Applying the Event Knowledge to Work Project Integration – Security Platform Evaluate Bedrock Agents to automate vulnerability scanning workflows
Apply agents to tasks such as log analysis and automated report generation
Use S3 Vector to store logs and vulnerability signatures
Improve retrieval performance while optimizing storage costs
Architecture Refinement Consider adopting Amazon Cognito for authentication and user management Apply a serverless-first approach to improve scalability and reduce operational overhead Event Experience Attending the AWS re:Invent 2025 Recap at the AWS Vietnam Office was a highly valuable experience during my internship.
Gaining Vision Helped clarify that Agentic AI is not a distant trend but a current direction in modern systems Live demonstrations provided insights into how AI agents can be applied in real scenarios Learning about Multimodal RAG expanded my understanding beyond text-based AI applications Technical Validation S3 Vector aligned well with our team’s need for a cost-efficient log analysis solution Discussions with AWS Solution Architects helped clarify IAM configurations and serverless best practices Community and Networking The event brought together developers and professionals sharing real-world challenges and solutions I had the opportunity to present the TheBois project and receive constructive feedback on system architecture Lessons Learned AI solutions should be designed as complete systems, not just models Prompt design and architecture both impact performance and cost Cost optimization is a critical factor in cloud system design Event Photos Add your event photos here
Overall Reflection This event not only provided updates on the latest AWS technologies but also delivered practical insights that can be directly applied to the Website Security Baseline Assessment Platform.
In particular, services such as Bedrock Agents and S3 Vector stand out as highly relevant for improving automation, intelligence, and cost efficiency in the system.

title: Authentication and RBAC
content:
Authentication and Role-Based Access Control In this section, we implement RBAC using AWS IAM.
Steps:
Create user group (Developers) & Attach AmazonS3ReadOnlyAccess policy Create IAM users (dev-1, dev-2) & Add users to the group Result:
Users inherit permissions from the group Permissions are managed centrally This demonstrates RBAC:
Users → Groups → Policies

title: Create User and Add to Group
content:
In this section, we will create an IAM User, assign the user to an existing group.
Objectives After completing this section, you will be able to:
Create an IAM User Assign the user to an existing group Step 1: Create IAM User & Add to Group Go to:
IAM Users Create user At this step:
Enter username: dev-2 Select: Provide user access to the AWS Management Console Choose: Add user to group Select group: test-policy Step 2: Complete User Creation After creation, the user dev-2 will appear in the user list. Step 3: Enable Console Access and Get Login Info Go to:
IAM > Users > dev-2 Do:
Enable Console access Choose Autogenerated password, Custom password or User sign-in with new password Copy Sign-in URL (this will use for User sign-in), username, and password

title: Proposal
content:
GuardScript — Code Protector Platform Project Proposal Team Members:
Full Name Student ID Role Võ Tấn Phát SE194484 Team Leader Bùi Minh Hiển SE190829 Member Dương Nguyên Bình SE194067 Member Trần Vinh SE193927 Member Nguyễn Duy Tùng SE196572 Member Nguyễn Đức Trí SE194091 Member 1. Executive Summary GuardScript is a script distribution platform with loader-based access control. Instead of distributing source code directly, the system serves script content through controlled endpoints with signature checks, timestamp/nonce validation, license enforcement, HWID binding, and workspace access policies.
The solution uses a serverless AWS architecture to reduce operational overhead, support scale, and satisfy cloud workshop objectives.
2. Problem Statement Current Problems The team identified three key issues in script delivery workflows:
Source code can be easily leaked or copied when shared manually Lack of fine-grained access control for users or teams Difficulty in tracking versions, access, and usage history Absence of centralized management leading to fragmented data Limited auditing, monitoring, and protection mechanisms Deployment and security systems are often complex and costly Solution The proposed system addresses these issues through:
Loader-based script retrieval instead of direct source distribution. Dual transfer protocols: v2 (XOR + response verification) and v3 (X25519 ECDH + AES-GCM). License/HWID controls with access list policies. Cloud-native observability using CloudWatch and application logs. Benefits and ROI Stronger control over script usage and distribution. Better protection against API abuse via signed requests and rate limits. Cost-efficient operation for student/demo workloads with pay-per-use services. 3. Solution Architecture The architecture follows a serverless model with edge delivery for frontend and Lambda-based API processing.
Typical request flow:
Client (browser / loader) → CloudFront Distribution (SSL termination, cache layer) → Static assets: S3 bucket (frontend) → API /api/*, /files/*: Lambda Function URL origin → DynamoDB (users, workspaces, projects, licenses, logs, rate_limits, ...) → S3 (script/content objects) → API Gateway WebSocket (real-time updates) → CloudWatch Logs / Alarms / Dashboard AWS Services Used Layer Service Details Runtime API AWS Lambda (Node.js 20.x) Modular monolith backend handlers Database Amazon DynamoDB Multi-table model, PAY_PER_REQUEST, TTL for temporary records Object Storage Amazon S3 Frontend hosting and content objects CDN & Edge Amazon CloudFront Static delivery and route behaviors for /api/*, /files/* Edge Security AWS WAF Protects against malicious web requests at CloudFront edge TLS/SSL AWS Certificate Manager Manages SSL/TLS certificates for HTTPS Notification Amazon SNS / SES Sends alerts and email notifications (invitations, alarms) Monitoring Amazon CloudWatch Alarms for errors/throttles/p95 duration + operational dashboard Real-time API Gateway WebSocket API Workspace/user/admin event broadcasting Delivery GitHub Actions + SAM Automated infrastructure and frontend deployment Component Design Client Layer: web dashboard and loader clients (Python/Node/Lua). Edge Layer: CloudFront routing and caching. Compute Layer: Lambda handlers for auth, workspace, project, license, and loader protocols. Data Layer: DynamoDB + S3. Observability Layer: CloudWatch metrics/alarms/dashboard and application logs. Monitoring & Observability CloudWatch alarms are configured for Errors, Throttles, and p95 Duration. A CloudWatch dashboard is provisioned for runtime visibility. Application-level logs are stored by workspace for auditability. Validation checklist includes auth, protocol, access-list, and alarm tests. 4. Technical Implementation 4.1. Technical Requirements Functional Requirements:
User registration, login, and session-based authentication. Workspace creation and management with role-based access control (owner, admin, member). Script project management: CRUD for projects, files, and bundled content. Script distribution via controlled loader endpoints (Protocol v2 and v3). License key management with HWID binding, expiration, and usage tracking. IP access control per workspace (blacklist and whitelist policies). Team collaboration via workspace invitations and membership management. Audit logging for all critical user and admin actions. Real-time event broadcasting via WebSocket API. Admin console for platform-level oversight and user management. Non-Functional Requirements:
Category Requirement Security HMAC-signed requests, ECDH key exchange, replay protection via nonce + timestamp Performance p95 API response < 500ms; CloudWatch alarms for latency breaches Scalability Serverless Lambda + DynamoDB on-demand auto-scales with load Availability CloudFront edge delivery; S3 high-durability object storage (99.999999999%) Maintainability Modular Lambda monolith; SAM/CloudFormation IaC for reproducible infrastructure Observability CloudWatch metrics, alarms, and dashboard; structured per-workspace application logs Technical Stack:
Component Technology Runtime Node.js 20.x on AWS Lambda Database Amazon DynamoDB (PAY_PER_REQUEST) Storage Amazon S3 Edge / CDN Amazon CloudFront + AWS WAF Real-time API Gateway WebSocket API Auth Custom HMAC-SHA256 token system Encryption AES-256-GCM, ECDH X25519, PBKDF2-SHA256 IaC AWS SAM / CloudFormation CI/CD GitHub Actions Monitoring Amazon CloudWatch (Logs, Alarms, Dashboard) 4.2. Implementation Phases The project follows Agile Scrum methodology with 6 sprints (1 week each):
Sprint 1 — Analysis & Architecture Design
Define system requirements Design AWS architecture Design database schema and APIs Sprint 2 — Backend Foundation
Set up Lambda project structure Implement Auth, User, Workspace APIs Integrate DynamoDB Sprint 3 — Script & File Management
CRUD for project, file, and content resources S3 integration for upload/download File tree structure and bundling Sprint 4 — Security & Access Control
Implement access control and licensing Integrate script protection loader (Protocol v2/v3) Build audit logging Sprint 5 — Frontend & Realtime
Develop dashboard UI Integrate APIs Configure WebSocket for real-time updates Sprint 6 — CI/CD & Deployment
Configure GitHub Actions pipeline Deploy via AWS SAM/CloudFormation Set up CloudWatch monitoring and alarms 5. Roadmap & Milestones Phase Content Timeline 1. Analysis & Architecture Design AWS onboarding, GuardScript scope definition, architecture design, DynamoDB schema, API planning Week 1–6 2. Backend Foundation Project kickoff, auth, workspace, project, file, and license APIs Week 7–8 3. Script, File & AWS Migration Encryption module, S3 integration, Lambda/DynamoDB/CloudFront setup, loader protocols Week 8–9 4. Security & Access Control Loader v2/v3 hardening, HWID lock, access-list policies, rate-limit validation Week 9–11 5. Frontend & Realtime Dashboard/workspace UI, WebSocket integration, responsive design, overall testing Week 7–10 6. CI/CD & Documentation SAM/CloudFormation deployment, CloudWatch monitoring, validation checklist, final reporting Week 11–12 6. Cost Estimation Typical monthly infrastructure cost (Free Tier / Small Scale): ~$4.32/month
Service Estimated Cost Notes AWS Lambda ~$0.00/month Free tier: 1M requests/month Amazon DynamoDB ~$0.60/month On-demand; 25 GB free storage Amazon S3 ~$0.80/month Frontend hosting + content storage Amazon CloudFront ~$0.77/month 1 TB free transfer (first year) Amazon CloudWatch ~$0.50/month 30-day log retention, alarms, dashboard API Gateway WebSocket ~$0.35/month WebSocket connections Amazon SES ~$0.09/month Email notifications and invitations Amazon SNS ~$0.00/month Alert notifications (mostly free tier) AWS WAF ~$0.21/month Edge protection rules AWS ACM ~$0.00/month SSL/TLS certificates (free for CloudFront) AWS IAM ~$0.00/month No direct cost Total ~$4.32/month Usage-based, serverless pay-per-use Lambda and DynamoDB are mostly covered by the free tier at low usage levels.
7. Risk Assessment Risk Impact Probability Mitigation Lambda cold start latency Medium Medium Optimize handlers and monitor p95 DynamoDB throttling High Low On-demand scaling, CloudWatch alerts S3 PUT/GET failures High Low Retry logic, versioning enabled Budget overrun Medium Low AWS Budgets alerts, optimize TTL/cache Replay attacks High Low Timestamp + nonce + HMAC required Contingency Plan:
If primary endpoint behavior fails → fallback to Lambda Function URL. If S3 is unavailable → retry with exponential backoff, log to CloudWatch. Use SAM/CloudFormation stacks for quick infrastructure recovery. 8. Expected Outcomes Technical Outcomes:
End-to-end AWS serverless deployment with validated API and loader flow. Dual loader protocol support with security controls (signature, HWID, rate-limit). Baseline observability for runtime health and performance. Reproducible deployment process aligned with workshop requirements. Long-term Value:
Extendable architecture for additional loaders and governance features. Reusable cloud-native reference for future FCJ workshop projects. 9. Future Improvements Add production-ready SES email workflow for invitations. Add AWS WAF managed rules for edge protection. Upgrade S3 encryption controls from SSE-S3 to SSE-KMS CMK where needed. Add AWS Budgets and Cost Anomaly Detection. Increase automated test coverage (unit/integration/security checks).

title: Event 2
content:
Summary Report: “AI Agents, Prompt Engineering, and AIoT Projects on AWS” Event Information Item Details Event Name AI Agents, Prompt Engineering, and AIoT Projects on AWS Date & Time 14/03/2026 Location AWS Vietnam Office (Floor 26 & 36), Ho Chi Minh City Role Attendee (FCJ Cloud Intern) Event Objectives Understand how AI Agents extend traditional LLM capabilities Learn prompt engineering techniques to improve output quality and efficiency Explore real-world AIoT architectures using AWS services Gain practical ideas for building AI-powered cloud applications Speakers Banh Cam Vinh – Topic: Building AI Agents with Strands Nguyen Tuan Thinh – DevOps Engineer, First Cloud AI Journey
Topic: Automated Prompt Engineering Aiden Dinh – Operation Engineer, Katalon
Topic: AIoT Projects Key Highlights AI Agents as the Next Step of AI Systems Traditional LLMs → Single-step responses AI Agents → Multi-step reasoning, tool usage, and decision-making Better suited for real-world workflows requiring automation Building AI Agents with Strands Core elements: system prompts, tool integration, knowledge base Workflow: input → reasoning → tool execution → response Simplifies development compared to manual orchestration Prompt Engineering and Optimization Important components: role, task, context, constraints, output format Best practices: Write clear and structured prompts Avoid ambiguity Separate logical sections Advanced techniques: Chain-of-Thought, RAG, role prompting Better prompts → improved accuracy and lower cost AIoT Architecture in Practice Use case: Locker Management System Hardware: Raspberry Pi, Arduino, RFID, sensors, camera AWS services: IoT Core → device communication Lambda → processing logic DynamoDB → storage Rekognition → identity recognition Goal: automate operations and improve efficiency Key Takeaways Shift Toward Agent-based AI AI is moving from prompt-based interaction to agent-driven systems Agents enable automation, reasoning, and integration Prompt Design Matters Prompt engineering directly affects output quality and cost Well-designed prompts improve consistency and reliability AWS Enables AIoT Systems AWS provides a full ecosystem for integrating AI with IoT Supports scalable and production-ready solutions Applying to Work Applying AI Agents Use agents for automation workflows such as log analysis and report generation Build multi-step AI features in system design Improving AI Output Apply structured prompting to improve response quality Reduce ambiguity and optimize token usage System Architecture Design Apply AIoT architecture concepts to future projects Integrate multiple AWS services into a unified system Event Experience Attending this event provided both conceptual understanding and practical insights into modern AI systems.
Learning New Concepts Gained a clearer understanding of AI Agents and their capabilities Recognized the importance of prompt engineering in real applications Real-world System Perspective AIoT project demonstrated how hardware and cloud services work together Provided a clear view of building end-to-end systems Practical Tools and Techniques Explored Strands for building AI Agents Learned advanced prompting techniques Lessons Learned AI systems require structured workflows, not just models Prompt quality impacts both performance and cost System integration is key in real-world applications Event Photos Add your event photos here
Overall Reflection This event provided valuable insights into AI Agents, prompt engineering, and AIoT system design. It highlighted how modern AI solutions require not only models, but also well-designed workflows and cloud integration.
The knowledge gained can be applied directly to future cloud and AI projects.

title: Access Denied and RBAC Validation
content:
In this section, we summarize the entire workshop and explain how the access control system works in AWS IAM using the Role-Based Access Control (RBAC) model.
Objectives After completing this section, you will be able to:
Understand the relationship between User, Group, Policy, and Resource Explain how users inherit permissions from groups Distinguish between allowed (Allow) and denied (Deny) actions Understand how AWS IAM applies the Least Privilege principle System Overview In this workshop, we implemented a basic access control model with the following components:
IAM User: dev-2 User Group: test-policy Policy: AmazonS3ReadOnlyAccess Resource: Amazon S3 System flow:
User (dev-2) → Group (test-policy) → Policy (AmazonS3ReadOnlyAccess) → Resource (S3) This means that user dev-2 is not assigned permissions directly. Instead, the user receives permissions through the group test-policy.
How the System Works AWS IAM in this workshop follows this model:
Create a User to represent a person or account Create a Group to represent a role Attach a Policy to the Group Add the User to the Group The User inherits all permissions from the Group This approach is important because:
Access control is managed centrally Multiple users can share the same permissions through one group It reduces the risk of configuration mistakes It aligns with the RBAC model Access Validation After finishing the configuration, we tested the real behavior of user dev-2.
Case 1: Allowed Actions User dev-2 was added to group test-policy, and the group was assigned the policy AmazonS3ReadOnlyAccess.
After logging in as dev-2, the results showed:
The user can access Amazon S3 The user can view the bucket list The user can open a bucket and read its data This confirms that:
The policy was applied successfully The user inherited permissions from the group Read-only access is working as expected Case 2: Denied Actions Next, we tested an action that requires write permission:
Attempt to upload a file to S3 Result:
The system returned Access Denied This shows that:
The user does not have s3:PutObject permission The current policy only allows read actions AWS IAM correctly blocks actions that exceed the granted permissions This is an important validation step because it proves that the system not only allows the correct actions, but also denies unauthorized ones.
Permission Analysis In this workshop, the AmazonS3ReadOnlyAccess policy allows:
Viewing the bucket list Viewing bucket contents Reading objects in S3 This policy does not allow:
Uploading files Deleting objects Modifying data Performing administrative actions Therefore, we can conclude:
User dev-2 has Read permission User dev-2 does not have Write permission Least Privilege Principle The system in this workshop follows the principle:
Grant only the minimum permissions required In this case:
The user needs access to S3 in order to view data The user does not need to upload, delete, or modify data Therefore, using AmazonS3ReadOnlyAccess is appropriate.
This principle is important because it:
Reduces security risks Limits the impact of user mistakes Makes the system safer and easier to control RBAC in AWS IAM RBAC is an access control model where:
Permissions are assigned to roles Users are assigned to roles Users inherit permissions from those roles In AWS IAM, this model maps as follows:
RBAC Component AWS IAM User IAM User Role User Group Permission Policy Resource AWS Service / Resource Flow:
User → Group → Policy → Resource So, in this workshop:
dev-2 is the User test-policy is the Role AmazonS3ReadOnlyAccess is the Permission Amazon S3 is the Resource Meaning of This Workshop This workshop is not only about creating users or attaching policies. It also simulates a real access control system.
Through this hands-on lab, we have:
Created a user to represent a real system user
Created a group to manage permissions by role
Attached a policy to control allowed actions
Logged in with a real IAM user to test permissions
Verified both scenarios:
Allow: access to S3 Deny: upload action blocked This demonstrates that the IAM configuration is correct and that the system behaves according to security expectations.
Conclusion After completing this workshop, we can conclude that:
Users do not need permissions attached directly Groups are an effective way to manage permissions centrally Policies clearly define what a user can and cannot do AWS IAM correctly applies the Least Privilege principle The RBAC model makes access control more scalable, organized, and secure

title: Workshop
content:
Authentication and Access Control using AWS IAM Overview AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM) is a service that enables secure control of access to AWS resources.
In this workshop, you will implement a complete access control workflow including:
Creating IAM users Creating user groups and attaching policies Assigning users to groups (RBAC) Enabling console access Logging in with IAM users Accessing Amazon S3 Testing permissions (read vs write) Observing Access Denied errors Objectives This workshop simulates a real-world authentication and authorization system:
Authentication: identifies who can access the system (IAM login) Authorization: defines what actions they can perform (IAM policies) You will also implement: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)
Where:
Users are assigned to Groups Groups are assigned Policies Users inherit permissions from Groups Hands-on Steps Create IAM Users (dev-1, dev-2) Create User Group (Developers) Attach AmazonS3ReadOnlyAccess policy to the group Add users to the group Enable console access and log in Access S3 bucket Attempt file upload → Access Denied Verify RBAC behavior Outcomes After completing this workshop, you will understand:
How AWS IAM manages access control How RBAC is implemented in practice The difference between read and write permissions How to troubleshoot Access Denied errors Content Workshop-overview Authentication-and-RBAC Workshop-overview Access-Denied-and-RBAC-Validation

title: Self-Assessment
content:
During my 12-week internship as a Cloud Intern (AWS), I had the opportunity to explore and practice with a wide range of AWS services, from foundational topics to more advanced areas such as serverless architecture, containers, DevOps, security, monitoring, and system optimization. This journey helped me expand not only my technical knowledge but also my ability to think about cloud systems in a more structured and practical way.
Throughout the internship, I worked with services such as EC2, S3, RDS, VPC, IAM, Lambda, API Gateway, DynamoDB, SQS, SNS, ECS, CloudWatch, CloudFormation, and CodePipeline. Through these activities, I strengthened my ability to learn new technologies, implement cloud-based solutions, understand how AWS services work together, and gradually develop a system-oriented mindset rather than studying each service in isolation.
In addition to technical growth, I also improved my self-learning ability, teamwork, openness to feedback, and capacity to connect technical knowledge with practical use cases. At the same time, I recognize that I still need to improve in several areas, especially technical depth, discipline, real-world problem solving, and professional communication.
To reflect on my internship period objectively, I evaluate myself based on the following criteria:
No. Criteria Description Good Fair Average 1 Professional knowledge & skills Understanding of AWS services, ability to apply knowledge in practice, and quality of implementation ✅ ☐ ☐ 2 Ability to learn Ability to absorb new technologies quickly, self-study, and adapt to new tasks ✅ ☐ ☐ 3 Proactiveness Taking initiative in learning, practicing, and completing assigned tasks ✅ ☐ ☐ 4 Sense of responsibility Completing work on time and maintaining quality ✅ ☐ ☐ 5 Discipline Maintaining a consistent working routine and following plans effectively ☐ ✅ ☐ 6 Progressive mindset Being open to feedback, recognizing weaknesses, and improving continuously ✅ ☐ ☐ 7 Communication Presenting ideas, reporting work, and discussing technical topics clearly ☐ ✅ ☐ 8 Teamwork Collaborating with others, sharing knowledge, and contributing to group work ✅ ☐ ☐ 9 Professional conduct Maintaining a serious attitude and respecting the work environment ✅ ☐ ☐ 10 Problem-solving skills Analyzing issues, proposing solutions, and understanding technical trade-offs ☐ ✅ ☐ 11 Contribution to project/team Contribution to progress, implementation, and system development direction ✅ ☐ ☐ 12 Overall General evaluation of the entire internship period ✅ ☐ ☐ Key Strengths Built a solid breadth of knowledge across important AWS service domains Learned quickly and connected multiple services into a broader system view Showed initiative in practicing and exploring beyond theoretical concepts Began developing a system design, DevOps, and cloud operations mindset Maintained a positive learning attitude and willingness to improve through feedback Areas for Improvement Need to go deeper into selected areas instead of remaining mostly broad in coverage Need to improve discipline and maintain a more consistent working rhythm Need to strengthen real-world problem-solving skills, especially in performance, cost, and operational issues Need to improve professional communication, particularly in presenting solutions and explaining technical choices Development Direction After the Internship Moving forward, I would like to continue growing toward a Cloud/DevOps Engineer path by improving technical depth, building more realistic hands-on projects, and strengthening my ability to design production-ready systems. I also aim to become better at analyzing trade-offs, optimizing systems, and communicating technical ideas more effectively in professional environments.

title: Week 5 – Serverless Computing
content:
Week 5 Objectives: Understand serverless architecture and its benefits. Learn how to create and manage AWS Lambda functions. Explore event triggers for Lambda. Configure API Gateway to build APIs. Work with DynamoDB as a NoSQL database. Build a REST API backend using serverless architecture. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material 02/02/2026 (Mon) - Introduction to serverless computing - Overview of AWS Lambda and use cases 02/02/2026 02/02/2026 https://cloudjourney.awsstudygroup.com/ 03/02/2026 (Tue) - Create and deploy Lambda functions - Implement basic function logic 03/02/2026 03/02/2026 https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda 04/02/2026 (Wed) - Configure Lambda event triggers - Integrate with S3 or API Gateway 04/02/2026 04/02/2026 https://docs.aws.amazon.com/lambda 05/02/2026 (Thu) - Introduction to API Gateway - Create and configure REST API endpoints 05/02/2026 05/02/2026 https://docs.aws.amazon.com/apigateway 06/02/2026 (Fri) - Learn DynamoDB - Perform CRUD operations (Create, Read, Update, Delete) 06/02/2026 06/02/2026 https://docs.aws.amazon.com/dynamodb 07/02/2026 (Sat) - Hands-on: Build REST API backend - Integrate Lambda + API Gateway + DynamoDB 07/02/2026 07/02/2026 https://cloudjourney.awsstudygroup.com/ Week 5 Achievements: Gained a solid understanding of serverless architecture and its advantages.
Successfully created and deployed AWS Lambda functions.
Configured event triggers for automated processing.
Built and configured REST APIs using API Gateway.
Worked effectively with DynamoDB and performed basic data operations.
Developed a complete REST API backend using serverless architecture.
Learned how to integrate multiple AWS services into a cohesive system.
Improved skills in building scalable and modern cloud applications.

title: Feedback & Suggestions
content:
This section is intended to capture personal reflections and feedback from participating in the First Cloud Journey program, as well as suggestions to help the FCJ team improve and enhance future experiences.
General Evaluation 1. Working Environment
The working environment is comfortable and welcoming. Team members are supportive and willing to help when needed, even outside of working hours. The workspace is organized, which helps maintain focus. However, organizing more team bonding or informal activities could further strengthen connections among members.
2. Support from Mentors / Admin Team
Mentors provide clear guidance and are always open to questions. They encourage independent problem-solving before offering direct solutions, which helps improve learning. The admin team also supports well in terms of documentation and processes, making the workflow smoother.
3. Relevance to Academic Background
The tasks assigned are generally aligned with what I have learned at university, while also introducing new areas. This balance helps reinforce foundational knowledge and expand practical experience.
4. Learning Opportunities & Skill Development
During the internship, I had the chance to develop various skills, including teamwork, task management, and professional communication. Insights shared by mentors also helped me better understand real-world applications and shape my career direction.
5. Culture & Team Spirit
The team culture is positive and collaborative. Members respect each other and work effectively together, especially during high-pressure situations. This made it easier for me to integrate into the team despite being an intern.
6. Internship Policies & Benefits
The program provides internship allowances and offers flexibility when needed. Additionally, access to internal training sessions is a valuable benefit that supports continuous learning.
Personal Reflection During my internship, what impressed me the most was the highly practical learning environment, where I was not only exposed to theoretical knowledge but also had the opportunity to directly implement and integrate multiple AWS services. In addition, the support from mentors and the willingness of experienced team members to share their knowledge made the environment more comfortable and motivating for continuous learning.
Suggestions & Expectations It would be beneficial to have a clearer learning roadmap for each stage, allowing interns to better track their progress and understand their development direction.
Additionally, organizing regular review sessions would help identify strengths and weaknesses more effectively, enabling timely improvement throughout the internship period.

title: Week 6 – Application Integration
content:
Week 6 Objectives: Understand AWS application integration services. Learn about Amazon SQS and SNS messaging services. Explore event routing using EventBridge. Learn how to build workflows with AWS Step Functions. Build an event-driven architecture. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material 09/02/2026 (Mon) - Introduction to AWS Application Integration - Overview of Amazon SQS and SNS 09/02/2026 09/02/2026 https://cloudjourney.awsstudygroup.com/ 10/02/2026 (Tue) - Work with Amazon SQS - Create queues and send/receive messages 10/02/2026 10/02/2026 https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sqs 11/02/2026 (Wed) - Learn Amazon SNS - Configure publish/subscribe model 11/02/2026 11/02/2026 https://docs.aws.amazon.com/sns 12/02/2026 (Thu) - Learn Amazon EventBridge - Configure event routing between services 12/02/2026 12/02/2026 https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eventbridge 13/02/2026 (Fri) - Introduction to AWS Step Functions - Build simple workflows 13/02/2026 13/02/2026 https://docs.aws.amazon.com/step-functions 14/02/2026 (Sat) - Hands-on: Build event-driven architecture - Integrate SQS, SNS, Lambda, and EventBridge 14/02/2026 14/02/2026 https://cloudjourney.awsstudygroup.com/ Week 6 Achievements: Gained a clear understanding of AWS application integration services.
Worked effectively with Amazon SQS for message queue processing.
Implemented publish/subscribe messaging using Amazon SNS.
Successfully configured EventBridge for event routing across services.
Built basic workflows using AWS Step Functions.
Developed an event-driven architecture integrating multiple AWS services.
Learned how AWS services interact in asynchronous systems.
Improved ability to design scalable and loosely coupled cloud architectures.

title: Week 7 – Containers & Orchestration
content:
Week 7 Objectives: Understand container concepts and Docker fundamentals. Learn how to build and manage container images. Explore Amazon ECS (Elastic Container Service). Work with Amazon ECR (Elastic Container Registry). Deploy a containerized web application on AWS. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material 16/02/2026 (Mon) - Introduction to containers and Docker - Install Docker and run first container 16/02/2026 16/02/2026 https://docs.docker.com 17/02/2026 (Tue) - Build Docker images - Work with Dockerfile - Manage local containers 17/02/2026 17/02/2026 https://docs.docker.com 18/02/2026 (Wed) - Introduction to Amazon ECS - Create clusters and services 18/02/2026 18/02/2026 https://docs.aws.amazon.com/ecs 19/02/2026 (Thu) - Learn Amazon ECR - Push and pull images from registry 19/02/2026 19/02/2026 https://docs.aws.amazon.com/ecr 20/02/2026 (Fri) - Integrate ECS with ECR - Deploy containers on ECS 20/02/2026 20/02/2026 https://docs.aws.amazon.com/ecs 21/02/2026 (Sat) - Hands-on: Deploy containerized web application - Test application and scale services 21/02/2026 21/02/2026 https://cloudjourney.awsstudygroup.com/ Week 7 Achievements: Gained a solid understanding of containerization and Docker fundamentals.
Successfully built and managed Docker images.
Learned how to use Amazon ECS for container orchestration.
Used Amazon ECR to store and manage container images.
Deployed a containerized application on AWS.
Understood integration between Docker and AWS services.
Improved skills in deploying microservices-based applications.
Enhanced ability to build scalable and flexible cloud systems.

title: Week 8 – Monitoring & Logging
content:
Week 8 Objectives: Understand monitoring and logging services in AWS. Use Amazon CloudWatch for metrics and alarms. Learn AWS CloudTrail for audit logging. Explore distributed tracing with AWS X-Ray. Build an end-to-end monitoring system. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material 23/02/2026 (Mon) - Introduction to monitoring & logging in AWS - Overview of Amazon CloudWatch 23/02/2026 23/02/2026 https://cloudjourney.awsstudygroup.com/ 24/02/2026 (Tue) - Work with CloudWatch metrics - Create dashboards and alarms 24/02/2026 24/02/2026 https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cloudwatch 25/02/2026 (Wed) - Learn AWS CloudTrail - Track system activity and changes 25/02/2026 25/02/2026 https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cloudtrail 26/02/2026 (Thu) - Introduction to AWS X-Ray - Trace requests across services 26/02/2026 26/02/2026 https://docs.aws.amazon.com/xray 27/02/2026 (Fri) - Analyze logs and traces - Combine CloudWatch, CloudTrail, and X-Ray 27/02/2026 27/02/2026 https://docs.aws.amazon.com 28/02/2026 (Sat) - Hands-on: Set up end-to-end monitoring system - Test alerts and logging 28/02/2026 28/02/2026 https://cloudjourney.awsstudygroup.com/ Week 8 Achievements: Gained a strong understanding of monitoring and logging in cloud environments.
Effectively used Amazon CloudWatch to monitor metrics and create alerts.
Tracked and analyzed system activity using AWS CloudTrail.
Applied AWS X-Ray for distributed request tracing.
Integrated multiple services to build a comprehensive monitoring system.
Improved ability to detect and troubleshoot system issues.
Enhanced operational skills for maintaining cloud systems.
Built a foundation for reliable and production-ready deployments.

title: Week 9 – Advanced Networking
content:
Week 9 Objectives: Understand advanced networking concepts in AWS. Learn about VPC Peering and AWS Transit Gateway. Explore AWS Direct Connect concepts. Configure content delivery using Amazon CloudFront. Build a global application architecture. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material 02/03/2026 (Mon) - Introduction to advanced networking - Overview of VPC Peering and Transit Gateway 02/03/2026 02/03/2026 https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc 03/03/2026 (Tue) - Configure VPC Peering - Connect multiple VPCs 03/03/2026 03/03/2026 https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc 04/03/2026 (Wed) - Learn AWS Transit Gateway - Design large-scale network architecture 04/03/2026 04/03/2026 https://docs.aws.amazon.com/vpc 05/03/2026 (Thu) - Introduction to AWS Direct Connect - Understand private connectivity with AWS 05/03/2026 05/03/2026 https://docs.aws.amazon.com/directconnect 06/03/2026 (Fri) - Learn Amazon CloudFront - Configure CDN for content delivery optimization 06/03/2026 06/03/2026 https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cloudfront 07/03/2026 (Sat) - Hands-on: Build global application architecture - Optimize performance and latency 07/03/2026 07/03/2026 https://cloudjourney.awsstudygroup.com/ Week 9 Achievements: Gained a strong understanding of advanced AWS networking concepts.
Configured VPC Peering to connect multiple private networks.
Understood the role of AWS Transit Gateway in large-scale network design.
Learned how AWS Direct Connect enables private connectivity.
Successfully configured Amazon CloudFront for content delivery acceleration.
Built a global application architecture with improved performance.
Enhanced skills in designing distributed and low-latency systems.
Prepared for building production-grade cloud networking solutions.

title: Week 10 – DevOps & CI/CD
content:
Week 10 Objectives: Understand DevOps and CI/CD concepts in AWS. Work with CodeCommit, CodeBuild, and CodeDeploy. Automate deployment pipelines using CodePipeline. Learn Infrastructure as Code (IaC) with CloudFormation. Build an automated deployment pipeline. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material 09/03/2026 (Mon) - Introduction to DevOps and CI/CD - Overview of AWS Code* services 09/03/2026 09/03/2026 https://cloudjourney.awsstudygroup.com/ 10/03/2026 (Tue) - Work with CodeCommit - Manage source code repositories 10/03/2026 10/03/2026 https://docs.aws.amazon.com/codecommit 11/03/2026 (Wed) - Use CodeBuild - Automate build and testing processes 11/03/2026 11/03/2026 https://docs.aws.amazon.com/codebuild 12/03/2026 (Thu) - Use CodeDeploy - Deploy applications to EC2/Lambda 12/03/2026 12/03/2026 https://docs.aws.amazon.com/codedeploy 13/03/2026 (Fri) - Configure CodePipeline - Automate CI/CD workflow 13/03/2026 13/03/2026 https://docs.aws.amazon.com/codepipeline 14/03/2026 (Sat) - Hands-on: Build automated deployment pipeline - Integrate CodeCommit + CodeBuild + CodeDeploy 14/03/2026 14/03/2026 https://cloudjourney.awsstudygroup.com/ Week 10 Achievements: Gained a solid understanding of DevOps and CI/CD practices in AWS.
Effectively used CodeCommit, CodeBuild, and CodeDeploy.
Built an automated CI/CD pipeline using CodePipeline.
Learned how to manage infrastructure using CloudFormation.
Automated application deployment processes.
Reduced manual errors in build and deployment workflows.
Improved skills in cloud-based DevOps practices.
Became ready to apply CI/CD in real-world projects.

title: Week 11 – Advanced Security
content:
Week 11 Objectives: Understand advanced security services in AWS. Learn about AWS WAF and AWS Shield for application protection. Manage secrets using AWS Secrets Manager. Use AWS Certificate Manager for SSL/TLS management. Build a secure production environment. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material 16/03/2026 (Mon) - Introduction to advanced AWS security - Overview of AWS WAF and AWS Shield 16/03/2026 16/03/2026 https://docs.aws.amazon.com/waf 17/03/2026 (Tue) - Configure AWS WAF - Set up rules to protect web applications 17/03/2026 17/03/2026 https://docs.aws.amazon.com/waf 18/03/2026 (Wed) - Learn AWS Shield - Protect against DDoS attacks 18/03/2026 18/03/2026 https://docs.aws.amazon.com/shield 19/03/2026 (Thu) - Work with AWS Secrets Manager - Store and manage sensitive information 19/03/2026 19/03/2026 https://docs.aws.amazon.com/secretsmanager 20/03/2026 (Fri) - Use AWS Certificate Manager - Configure SSL/TLS for applications 20/03/2026 20/03/2026 https://docs.aws.amazon.com/acm 21/03/2026 (Sat) - Hands-on: Secure production environment - Combine WAF, SSL, and secrets 21/03/2026 21/03/2026 https://cloudjourney.awsstudygroup.com/ Week 11 Achievements: Gained a strong understanding of advanced AWS security services.
Configured AWS WAF to protect applications from common web threats.
Learned how AWS Shield mitigates DDoS attacks.
Used AWS Secrets Manager to securely manage sensitive data.
Implemented SSL/TLS using AWS Certificate Manager.
Built a secure production-ready environment following best practices.
Improved awareness of cloud security principles.
Became capable of deploying highly secure AWS systems.

title: Week 12 – Optimization & Best Practices
content:
Week 12 Objectives: Understand cost optimization strategies in AWS. Learn performance tuning techniques. Explore disaster recovery planning. Apply AWS best practices in system design. Build a production-ready architecture. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material 23/03/2026 (Mon) - Introduction to system optimization - Overview of cost optimization strategies 23/03/2026 23/03/2026 https://docs.aws.amazon.com/wellarchitected 24/03/2026 (Tue) - Analyze costs - Apply cost optimization techniques (rightsizing, reserved instances) 24/03/2026 24/03/2026 https://docs.aws.amazon.com 25/03/2026 (Wed) - Optimize system performance - Use caching and scaling strategies 25/03/2026 25/03/2026 https://docs.aws.amazon.com 26/03/2026 (Thu) - Learn disaster recovery strategies - Backup and failover models 26/03/2026 26/03/2026 https://docs.aws.amazon.com 27/03/2026 (Fri) - Apply AWS Well-Architected Framework - Evaluate system based on best practices 27/03/2026 27/03/2026 https://docs.aws.amazon.com/wellarchitected 28/03/2026 (Sat) - Hands-on: Build production-ready architecture - Optimize cost, performance, and availability 28/03/2026 28/03/2026 https://cloudjourney.awsstudygroup.com/ Week 12 Achievements: Gained a strong understanding of cost optimization strategies.
Applied performance tuning techniques to improve system efficiency.
Learned disaster recovery approaches to ensure high availability.
Applied AWS Well-Architected Framework principles.
Built a production-ready architecture meeting performance, cost, and reliability requirements.
Improved ability to evaluate and optimize cloud systems.
Completed the internship with comprehensive AWS knowledge.
Became ready to design and deploy real-world cloud solutions.
